title: When Genius Failed: The Rise and Fall of Long-Term Capital Management
excerpt: The definitive account of Long-Term Capital Management's meteoric rise and catastrophic collapse — a cautionary tale of financial genius, hubris, and the dangers of leverage that nearly brought down the global financial system.
keyIdeas: LTCM was founded by John Meriwether and staffed with Nobel laureates and the brightest minds in finance
The fund employed massive leverage — exceeding $100 billion in assets — to exploit tiny arbitrage opportunities
LTCM's models failed because they could not account for extreme market dislocations and correlated risk
The 1998 Russian default triggered a chain reaction that destroyed LTCM and threatened global markets
keyTakeaways: Leverage is the single greatest risk factor in financial markets
Mathematical models cannot fully capture the complexity and unpredictability of markets
Even the brightest minds can be wrong when they underestimate tail risk
The Federal Reserve's orchestrated bailout of LTCM set a dangerous precedent for too-big-to-fail
whoShouldRead: Finance professionals and risk managers wanting to understand leverage disasters
Students of financial history and the 1998 crisis
Investors who want to understand the dangers of overconfidence in quantitative models
General readers interested in a superb financial narrative
whoShouldSkip: Readers seeking hedge fund trading strategies to copy
Those who prefer interviews over narrative history
People looking for a defense of quantitative finance
